title: Document metadata
summary: Set machine-readable title, authors, description, keywords, and creation date with a top-level document set rule.
example:
#set document(
  title: [Collaborative Typesetting Study],
  author: "TOSS Community Team",
  date: datetime(year: 2026, month: 7, day: 15),
  keywords: ("TOSS", "Typst", "collaboration"),
)

#title()
notes: Place the set rule at the document top level before document content.
document.title is content; document.author and document.keywords accept a string or an array of strings.
The title element renders the configured title; setting metadata alone does not visibly render it.

title: Content and strings
summary: Keep markup content distinct from plain strings and construct the type required by each API.
notes: Square brackets create content; quoted values create strings.
Do not replace a required string or array-of-strings parameter with content merely because it renders similarly.
Typst 0.15 has no general str(content) conversion; retain the original string when both representations are needed.

title: Set and show rules
summary: Use set rules for element properties and show rules for transformations; place rules before the content they affect.
example:
#set text(size: 11pt)
#show heading.where(level: 1): set text(fill: blue)

= Styled heading
notes: Named arguments use a colon, not an equals sign.
A set rule only affects following content in its scope.

title: Arrays and dictionaries
summary: Use parenthesized comma-separated values for arrays and named entries for dictionaries.
notes: A one-element array needs a trailing comma.
Dictionary entries use name: value syntax.

title: Imports and includes
summary: Import definitions when names are needed and include a file when its produced content should appear in the document.
notes: Relative paths resolve from the file containing the import or include.
Use import for definitions and include for document content.
